\[Header graphic: A double-lined rectangular border encloses the text "__PH_0__" in large, bold, italicized sans-serif type, followed by "The Pacific Northwest Orienteering News for November/December 1998" in an elegant cursive script, and "Produced by Cascade Orienteering Club" right-aligned at the bottom.\]

Editor on Leave

Don Denbo is taking a two issue break from his duties as editor of __PH_0__ in order to focus on the Washington Interscholastic School League, which runs from November through February. In turn, Bruce McAlister has agreed to produce the January/February issue, while I, Rick Hood, am doing this one. (My task became somewhat more challenging when my computer’s hard drive DIED last week; hence the more simple formatting in this issue. Moreover, my apologies if you sent something in via email and it doesn’t appear in this issue; please send it to Bruce for the next issue.)

November 7th Event Now at Volunteer Park

COC’s November 7th event, originally scheduled for Magnuson Park, will now be held at VOLUNTEER Park, as WIOL organizers requested we save Magnuson for the Feb Championship Event.

> __PH_1__ is produced by Cascade Orienteering Club, a 501.c3 non-profit organization promoting and hosting orienteering events in Western Washington. COC achieves this aim by working closely with the other orienteering clubs in the region.
>
> All contributions are welcome and are best sent via an email text message. Items for the January/February issue must be received by Bruce McAlister by DECEMBER 25. His e-mail address is: 70673.1764@compuserve.com. You may also send typed items to COC at POB 31375, Seattle WA 98103. Any item in a newsletter may be reproduced for promotion of the sport.

WIOL & Winter O Season Getting Underway

Director Dave Tallent and Registrar/Results Chief Don Denbo are gearing up for another great WIOL Season.

The first practice event is on November 7th at Volunteer Park in Seattle, with the first official event being held on the recently-updated Farrel-McWhirter Park map on Dec 5th. There are an additional 6 season meets before the Championships on February 20th at Magnuson Park. [Please see the schedule for all the details, or better yet, please visit the WIOL webpage at www.eskimo.com/~halter/orienteering/wiol/index.html]

Along with the WIOL season, Debbie Newell and COC are pleased to host the Winter O’ Series, which is open to all adult orienteers. The series meets are the same as the regular season WIOL meets and are indicated on the schedule page with a “❄”

The COC Annual Dinner – All Welcome

On Sunday, December 13th you are welcome to come and join in the fun of Cascade Orienteering Club’s Annual Meeting featuring: the “two-minute election”; a preview of 1999 in O; recognition of outstanding volunteers, and fun and games.

The Bog Slog – A Different Event This Year

The annual COC “between Christmas and New Year’s Day” event at St. Edward St. Park will sport a different look this year. There will be two trail courses with the route marked with flagging. The shorter course will have approximately 4-6 controls along the route and the longer course will have approximately 8-12 controls along the route. (The longer course will actually be an extension of the short course.) Anyone is, of course, welcome to run the entire event on the trails. However, many will find it advantageous to go off-trail between some controls. The choice is yours. Mass start for the longer course is at 11:00 and for the shorter course is at 11:30 am.

HOWEVER, we know when not to fool with tradition; the winners will be awarded home-made pies and all are welcome to the post event pot luck to be held again at Bruce McAlister’s home within a few kilometers of the park. (THANKS Bruce.)

So, don’t miss this traditional event with a new twist; it should be a lot of fun!!!

Nisqually Notes

By Carl Moore

Please note that the Dec 19th meet put on by Nisqually will be at Fort Steilacoom Park and not Spanaway Park as noted on the WSOA schedule. Dave and Debbie wanted to use Spanaway but I walked through it recently and there have been too many terrain changes for a suitable meet there. We will use the old standby Steilacoom instead. Are there any volunteers to upgrade the Steilacoom and Spanaway maps out there? By the way, the Fishtrap mapping is coming along on schedule. We hope to get it completely done before the serious shooting starts. (Deer hunting season). Scott Donald will then begin OCADing it this winter.

On Saturday, January 16, 1999, Nisqually Orienteers will hold its annual Rambo competition at Point Defiance Park in Tacoma. The mass start for this 3-hour score O will be at 10:30 am. This yearly event manages to combine the worst weather of the winter with the thickest vegetation in Western Washington. However, it draws a large crowd of participants. Last year there were over 120 competitors. Eileen and Rick Breseman won the Rambo trophy, edging out a large number of veteran orienteers. Teams of two or more are encouraged, but individuals may compete. School kids are welcome but those younger than high school age are going to have a tough time of it. Rambo is successful thanks to the efforts of the Whaleys, the Miners, Ken Peters and others besides the Moores.

Impressions of Kamchatka (August 1998)

by Harvey Friedman

[These are the opening sentences of an approximately ten page article written by Harvey after his big adventure this summer. The entire article, along with selective links, appears on Howard’s webpage at: http://www.eskimo.com/~halter/orienteering/pnw.html]

In case anyone wonders about why I haven't kept in touch, I have my reasons. Because [my wife] Anne works for the National Marine Mammal Laboratory and has done much work with northern fur seals, she had the opportunity to travel to the Commander Islands, off the Kamchatka peninsula in the Russian far east. By reorganizing my own work load, I was able to take vacation to accompany her. I purchased a ticket on Alaska Air rather than Aeroflot and reserved a seat next to Anne. Then it was finish up work assignments and try to finish anything necessary around the house.

We had hoped that the contractors that we hired to remove the old and nail on a new roof would be finished before we left. They didn't finish and contributed to making my trip less enjoyable than it should have been. They didn't clean up each day as they had led us to believe they would and I discovered that old asphalt particles from composition shingles act like ball bearings if they are stepped on. One evening as I stepped from the front porch, my foot slipped at the very top step and I slid and/or bounced down the 8 stairs as if I came off a luge (for those fans of the Winter Olympics). Having heard a crack, thud, crunch, I was scared that I had seriously injured myself and wouldn't be able to go to Russia and slowly asked my body what hurt the most. I pulled myself to my feet. … [Please see the webpage for the rest of the story. – ed]

CROC and The World Masters Games

by John Bartholomew
CROC member & WMG O’ Media Liaison

[John’s excellent article will also appear in full at Howard’s website. What follows are the opening paragraphs– ed.]

Portland, OR & Goldendale, WA, August 10-14, 1998

If you like it hot, you should have been orienteering in the Pacific Northwest this summer! Our heat wave continued from the World Rogaine Champs to the US Relay Champs and right on into mid-August for the 1998 Nike World Masters Games in Portland, Oregon. The World Masters Games involved over eleven thousand athletes from around the world participating in twenty-five sports over a two week period. Four days of top-notch advanced orienteering were included, but only for participants thirty-five years and older. The first two days' events were held in the Portland, OR area, while the two day classic event was held at a site two hours' drive from Portland, on the drier eastern side of the Cascade mountain range in Washington state.

Day 1 (August 10th) was a training event day, held in the Hoyt Arboretum of Washington Park in Portland, Oregon. The area is quite varied: moderately steep ridge and valley terrain with an interesting mix of vegetation ranging from manicured lawns, formal plantings, city streets, groupings of specimen plants and second growth Pacific Northwest rainforest. A few nettles and blackberry patches exist, but no poison oak. The forests are fairly typical of the west side of the Cascade Mountains where our abundant rainfall nourishes big trees and usually dense undergrowth. This park was mapped in 1996 by John Britton from the United Kingdom. Over half the registered orienteering athletes attended the training day event.

Another orienteering opportunity available was the “See Portland” orienteering course, using the Portland Tri-Met transit system map. The course was set up in Score-O style, with participants using their feet and the Tri-Met mass transit system to visit the marked locations around town. (A free monthly transit pass was given to all WMG athletes and volunteers.) This “Bus-O” course was available to the public for the duration of the Games.

Day 2 (August 11th) saw the short course event at Powell Butte Nature Park in Portland, Oregon. Powell Butte is just that - a butte, sitting several hundred feet above Portland, offering incredible vistas of downtown Portland and the Cascade Range, including Mt. Hood, Mt. St. Helens and Mt. Adams. It's a favorite location for mountain bikers, hikers and wildlife watchers. Powell Butte is physically challenging, due more to the vegetation than the terrain. The park is a large broad hill, most of which has been cleared. The open area is infested with hawthorn, a shrubby tree covered with wicked thorns. Himalayan blackberry patches also dot the open area, and are often impenetrable. The open fields on the butte are often full with hip high grass. The sides of the butte are forested, and contain some nettles. At least there's no poison oak at this park. The Powell Butte map was updated in 1997 by George Kirkov from Bulgaria.

Competition was strong in the M50A and M55A classes, where only a few minutes separated the top competitors. In the M60B class, the gold medal winner (Udo Grady) was less than a minute ahead of his rival (Bob Reddick, SAMM). Udo Grady still managed this victory after sustaining a back injury while competing in a WMG Track and Field event several days earlier! (see Bob Reddick's article in the October 1998 ONA for more details). Sharon Crawford took the gold in the F50A category, while Sharon's mother, Ruth Crawford, participated as the oldest orienteering athlete, at age 88. Ruth finished her course, but completed it over the two hour time limit. ESPN devoted four hours of air time in August to the World Masters Games, and orienteering had its share of the limelight. A video crew taped the short course competition, and a five minute segment was aired during one of the WMG time slots on ESPN. Peter Snell (former Olympic track and field medalist from New Zealand; NTOC member) and his wife Miki were the main focus of the ESPN segment. [Peter ran his course wearing a wireless microphone!] One local newspaper covered the short course event, as well.

Days 3 and 4 (August 13th and 14th) of the competition were a 2-day classic event, held two hours east of Portland up the Columbia River Gorge in Goldendale, WA. Being on the dry side of the Cascade Mountains, and the edge of the central Oregon/Washington high desert, the competitors found second growth Ponderosa Pine and Oak forest with some meadows. The top of the map area is quite open and runnable, although lots of small volcanic stones hidden in the low grass can give a nasty ankle sprain. Temperatures on the east side of the Cascades run considerably higher in the summer than those two hours' drive west in Portland. Brooks Memorial State Park and the adjacent Boise-Cascade timber land were mapped in 1997 by George Kirkov, especially for this event. The map is 1:10000 scale, printed on 21"x14" paper. Many good comments were received on the quality of the map. Unfortunately, a last-minute glitch in the printing of the nine courses onto the Brooks Memorial State Park maps left the WMG volunteer crew drawing the courses on by hand the night before and the morning of the first days at Brooks. As a result, the first day of the two day classic started over two hours later than planned, pushing the competition into hotter daytime temperatures (topping 100 F). In addition, sufficient water controls were not arranged for the first day. These two oversights combined with the hot, dry weather resulted in longer times for most competitors, and several DNFs. One competitor collapsed from heat exhaustion after crossing the finish line, and was taken by ambulance to the local hospital. He was treated and released, and returned to compete the second day (and still won a medal!).
[Please visit Howard’s PNWO website to read the remainder of this excellent piece. –ed.]

Ride-Like-The-Wind Bike O

Edmonds, WA
September 13, 1998

* Meet Staff: Rick Hood with Debbie Newell assisting

The format was a 45 minute score-O with the control being worth from 1 to 3 million points apiece.
We now have a basic map that can be used for future bike O event or even go into our "trim course" offerings.

Rooks Park, Walla Walla, WA

Sunday, September 20, 1998
Sacajawea Orienteers (SACO)

__PH_0__ Frank Skorina Control Pickup: Holly, Michel, Steve, Liliane
* Participants: 22

The second-year medical students deliberated whether to compete against each other on the intermediate or advanced course. They had experience with CROC orange level courses and Brett had done at least one green level course. Since they had driven all the way from Portland, they decided to maximize their orienteering and go for the longer advanced course. Brett started two minutes in front of Erik and after meeting up in the woods the friendship developed into high-tempo, head-to-head competition. At the line they finished simultaneously in a near-sprint and were one-two on the advanced course. Chris Howard (along with Penny, his small, but tough dog) rounded out the top three having warmed up with a seven mile run in the morning. Congratulations to the sleep-deprived med students.

Weather, although threatening for a moment, was great and turnout was good considering that this was the first meet of the fall and there was minimal publicity to remind the population of that fact. Most of the participants are members which reflects our membership growth. We are at 32 members (once everyone who promised they will renew does) which is almost 50% higher than our Dec. 15, 1997 level.

Wallula East

October 18, 1998
Sacajawea OC

__PH_0__ Ivar Husa Control Pickup: Bob Gruel and Sonny Elsasser
* Participants: 15

It was as close to a perfect day for orienteering as one could expect. Sunshine, temperatures in the 60's, and no wind. Oh, and two water controls for the longest (8.5km) course! Participants could choose among four courses, a first for our club. We may break down and begin rating them by color, instead of "Beginner, Intermediate, and Advanced".

A note on the map. This was the first use of “Wallula East”, a 1:15000 subset of a larger mapped area, nearly 6 km long! Mapping continues on the south shore.

Vampire O

October 25
Woodland Park
Cascade Orienteering Club

__PH_0__ Robert Miller Assistant Meet Director: Sandy Kish Miller
__PH_2__ Jim Siscel Course Pickup: Jim Siscel, Paul Magnus, Bob & Pat Reddick
* Shadow: Bill Cusworth

This year we had a lovely Fall evening for the Vampire O, no rain and just cool enough to keep the runners from overheating. As in past years, it is utterly unpredictable as to who will come in with the most points. Two fast runners, Paul Magnus and Eric Bone, in the past have been bit many times, losing many points. This year, both runners came prepared with their strategies, Paul wore dark sweats and Eric came in disguise.

Unfortunately Eric's disguise involved sauntering and this lost him valuable minutes. Judging by the times of some participants, I think some runners wanted time to get to some points w/o running into vampires - or perhaps they just were having a great time and didn't want it to end.

Michael Thomas won the award for the best costume. I am not quite sure what he was meant to be, but the costume was suitably horror filled. (I am sure he is really a very nice person.)
Robert Miller

> Note: Club contacts and membership forms, USOF information, and other regular features will return next issue. Should you need any information before then, please contact one of the meet directors or leave a message on the Orienteering Hotline at 206.783.3966.

COC’s Annual Ballot for Board Members

Please select five (5) individuals from the list provided or write in selections of your own choice. In order for your ballot to count, you must print and sign your name in the space provided. You may mail your ballot to the COC address (POB 31375, Seattle, WA 98103) such that it arrives by November 11th or bring it to the annual dinner.

You are welcome to photocopy this form.

block_index.text: \[Header graphic: A double-lined rectangular border encloses the text "BEARING 315" in large, bold, italicized sans-serif type, followed by "The Pacific Northwest Orienteering News for November/December 1998" in an elegant cursive script, and "Produced by Cascade Orienteering Club" right-aligned at the bottom.\]

Don Denbo is taking a two issue break from his duties as editor of BEARING 315 in order to focus on the Washington Interscholastic School League, which runs from November through February. In turn, Bruce McAlister has agreed to produce the January/February issue, while I, Rick Hood, am doing this one. (My task became somewhat more challenging when my computer’s hard drive DIED last week; hence the more simple formatting in this issue. Moreover, my apologies if you sent something in via email and it doesn’t appear in this issue; please send it to Bruce for the next issue.)
November 7th Event Now at Volunteer Park
COC’s November 7th event, originally scheduled for Magnuson Park, will now be held at VOLUNTEER Park, as WIOL organizers requested we save Magnuson for the Feb Championship Event.

> BEARING 315 is produced by Cascade Orienteering Club, a 501.c3 non-profit organization promoting and hosting orienteering events in Western Washington. COC achieves this aim by working closely with the other orienteering clubs in the region. > > All contributions are welcome and are best sent via an email text message. Items for the January/February issue must be received by Bruce McAlister by DECEMBER 25. His e-mail address is: 70673.1764@compuserve.com. You may also send typed items to COC at POB 31375, Seattle WA 98103. Any item in a newsletter may be reproduced for promotion of the sport.
WIOL & Winter O Season Getting Underway
Director Dave Tallent and Registrar/Results Chief Don Denbo are gearing up for another great WIOL Season.

The first practice event is on November 7th at Volunteer Park in Seattle, with the first official event being held on the recently-updated Farrel-McWhirter Park map on Dec 5th. There are an additional 6 season meets before the Championships on February 20th at Magnuson Park. [Please see the schedule for all the details, or better yet, please visit the WIOL webpage at www.eskimo.com/~halter/orienteering/wiol/index.html]

Along with the WIOL season, Debbie Newell and COC are pleased to host the Winter O’ Series, which is open to all adult orienteers. The series meets are the same as the regular season WIOL meets and are indicated on the schedule page with a “❄”
The COC Annual Dinner – All Welcome

On Sunday, December 13th you are welcome to come and join in the fun of Cascade Orienteering Club’s Annual Meeting featuring: the “two-minute election”; a preview of 1999 in O; recognition of outstanding volunteers, and fun and games.
The Bog Slog – A Different Event This Year

The annual COC “between Christmas and New Year’s Day” event at St. Edward St. Park will sport a different look this year. There will be two trail courses with the route marked with flagging. The shorter course will have approximately 4-6 controls along the route and the longer course will have approximately 8-12 controls along the route. (The longer course will actually be an extension of the short course.) Anyone is, of course, welcome to run the entire event on the trails. However, many will find it advantageous to go off-trail between some controls. The choice is yours. Mass start for the longer course is at 11:00 and for the shorter course is at 11:30 am.

HOWEVER, we know when not to fool with tradition; the winners will be awarded home-made pies and all are welcome to the post event pot luck to be held again at Bruce McAlister’s home within a few kilometers of the park. (THANKS Bruce.)
So, don’t miss this traditional event with a new twist; it should be a lot of fun!!!

Please note that the Dec 19th meet put on by Nisqually will be at Fort Steilacoom Park and not Spanaway Park as noted on the WSOA schedule. Dave and Debbie wanted to use Spanaway but I walked through it recently and there have been too many terrain changes for a suitable meet there. We will use the old standby Steilacoom instead. Are there any volunteers to upgrade the Steilacoom and Spanaway maps out there? By the way, the Fishtrap mapping is coming along on schedule. We hope to get it completely done before the serious shooting starts. (Deer hunting season). Scott Donald will then begin OCADing it this winter.

On Saturday, January 16, 1999, Nisqually Orienteers will hold its annual Rambo competition at Point Defiance Park in Tacoma. The mass start for this 3-hour score O will be at 10:30 am. This yearly event manages to combine the worst weather of the winter with the thickest vegetation in Western Washington. However, it draws a large crowd of participants. Last year there were over 120 competitors. Eileen and Rick Breseman won the Rambo trophy, edging out a large number of veteran orienteers. Teams of two or more are encouraged, but individuals may compete. School kids are welcome but those younger than high school age are going to have a tough time of it. Rambo is successful thanks to the efforts of the Whaleys, the Miners, Ken Peters and others besides the Moores.

[These are the opening sentences of an approximately ten page article written by Harvey after his big adventure this summer. The entire article, along with selective links, appears on Howard’s webpage at: http://www.eskimo.com/~halter/orienteering/pnw.html]

In case anyone wonders about why I haven't kept in touch, I have my reasons. Because [my wife] Anne works for the National Marine Mammal Laboratory and has done much work with northern fur seals, she had the opportunity to travel to the Commander Islands, off the Kamchatka peninsula in the Russian far east. By reorganizing my own work load, I was able to take vacation to accompany her. I purchased a ticket on Alaska Air rather than Aeroflot and reserved a seat next to Anne. Then it was finish up work assignments and try to finish anything necessary around the house.

We had hoped that the contractors that we hired to remove the old and nail on a new roof would be finished before we left. They didn't finish and contributed to making my trip less enjoyable than it should have been. They didn't clean up each day as they had led us to believe they would and I discovered that old asphalt particles from composition shingles act like ball bearings if they are stepped on. One evening as I stepped from the front porch, my foot slipped at the very top step and I slid and/or bounced down the 8 stairs as if I came off a luge (for those fans of the Winter Olympics). Having heard a crack, thud, crunch, I was scared that I had seriously injured myself and wouldn't be able to go to Russia and slowly asked my body what hurt the most. I pulled myself to my feet. … [Please see the webpage for the rest of the story. – ed]
CROC and The World Masters Games
by John Bartholomew CROC member & WMG O’ Media Liaison
[John’s excellent article will also appear in full at Howard’s website. What follows are the opening paragraphs– ed.]

If you like it hot, you should have been orienteering in the Pacific Northwest this summer! Our heat wave continued from the World Rogaine Champs to the US Relay Champs and right on into mid-August for the 1998 Nike World Masters Games in Portland, Oregon. The World Masters Games involved over eleven thousand athletes from around the world participating in twenty-five sports over a two week period. Four days of top-notch advanced orienteering were included, but only for participants thirty-five years and older. The first two days' events were held in the Portland, OR area, while the two day classic event was held at a site two hours' drive from Portland, on the drier eastern side of the Cascade mountain range in Washington state.

Day 1 (August 10th) was a training event day, held in the Hoyt Arboretum of Washington Park in Portland, Oregon. The area is quite varied: moderately steep ridge and valley terrain with an interesting mix of vegetation ranging from manicured lawns, formal plantings, city streets, groupings of specimen plants and second growth Pacific Northwest rainforest. A few nettles and blackberry patches exist, but no poison oak. The forests are fairly typical of the west side of the Cascade Mountains where our abundant rainfall nourishes big trees and usually dense undergrowth. This park was mapped in 1996 by John Britton from the United Kingdom. Over half the registered orienteering athletes attended the training day event.

Another orienteering opportunity available was the “See Portland” orienteering course, using the Portland Tri-Met transit system map. The course was set up in Score-O style, with participants using their feet and the Tri-Met mass transit system to visit the marked locations around town. (A free monthly transit pass was given to all WMG athletes and volunteers.) This “Bus-O” course was available to the public for the duration of the Games.

Day 2 (August 11th) saw the short course event at Powell Butte Nature Park in Portland, Oregon. Powell Butte is just that - a butte, sitting several hundred feet above Portland, offering incredible vistas of downtown Portland and the Cascade Range, including Mt. Hood, Mt. St. Helens and Mt. Adams. It's a favorite location for mountain bikers, hikers and wildlife watchers. Powell Butte is physically challenging, due more to the vegetation than the terrain. The park is a large broad hill, most of which has been cleared. The open area is infested with hawthorn, a shrubby tree covered with wicked thorns. Himalayan blackberry patches also dot the open area, and are often impenetrable. The open fields on the butte are often full with hip high grass. The sides of the butte are forested, and contain some nettles. At least there's no poison oak at this park. The Powell Butte map was updated in 1997 by George Kirkov from Bulgaria.

Competition was strong in the M50A and M55A classes, where only a few minutes separated the top competitors. In the M60B class, the gold medal winner (Udo Grady) was less than a minute ahead of his rival (Bob Reddick, SAMM). Udo Grady still managed this victory after sustaining a back injury while competing in a WMG Track and Field event several days earlier! (see Bob Reddick's article in the October 1998 ONA for more details). Sharon Crawford took the gold in the F50A category, while Sharon's mother, Ruth Crawford, participated as the oldest orienteering athlete, at age 88. Ruth finished her course, but completed it over the two hour time limit. ESPN devoted four hours of air time in August to the World Masters Games, and orienteering had its share of the limelight. A video crew taped the short course competition, and a five minute segment was aired during one of the WMG time slots on ESPN. Peter Snell (former Olympic track and field medalist from New Zealand; NTOC member) and his wife Miki were the main focus of the ESPN segment. [Peter ran his course wearing a wireless microphone!] One local newspaper covered the short course event, as well.

Days 3 and 4 (August 13th and 14th) of the competition were a 2-day classic event, held two hours east of Portland up the Columbia River Gorge in Goldendale, WA. Being on the dry side of the Cascade Mountains, and the edge of the central Oregon/Washington high desert, the competitors found second growth Ponderosa Pine and Oak forest with some meadows. The top of the map area is quite open and runnable, although lots of small volcanic stones hidden in the low grass can give a nasty ankle sprain. Temperatures on the east side of the Cascades run considerably higher in the summer than those two hours' drive west in Portland. Brooks Memorial State Park and the adjacent Boise-Cascade timber land were mapped in 1997 by George Kirkov, especially for this event. The map is 1:10000 scale, printed on 21"x14" paper. Many good comments were received on the quality of the map. Unfortunately, a last-minute glitch in the printing of the nine courses onto the Brooks Memorial State Park maps left the WMG volunteer crew drawing the courses on by hand the night before and the morning of the first days at Brooks. As a result, the first day of the two day classic started over two hours later than planned, pushing the competition into hotter daytime temperatures (topping 100 F). In addition, sufficient water controls were not arranged for the first day. These two oversights combined with the hot, dry weather resulted in longer times for most competitors, and several DNFs. One competitor collapsed from heat exhaustion after crossing the finish line, and was taken by ambulance to the local hospital. He was treated and released, and returned to compete the second day (and still won a medal!). [Please visit Howard’s PNWO website to read the remainder of this excellent piece. –ed.]

> Note: Each of the meet results formats below reflects the individual style of the respective meet director. We not only encourage MDs to send in their results, but also write a description of memorable moments at the meet– ed.
August 30, 1998 – Cle Elum, WA Sammamish Orienteering Club
Meet Director: Dave Enger Course Setter: Mark Howlett Registration: Dave Enger Timing: Mark Howlett Control Pick-up: Mark Howlett
Meet Staff: Rick Hood with Debbie Newell assisting

The format was a 45 minute score-O with the control being worth from 1 to 3 million points apiece. We now have a basic map that can be used for future bike O event or even go into our "trim course" offerings.
Results for those competing on Bike are:
Results for those competing on foot on the same course (where foot competitors received double points) are:

Thanks to Dave Tallent and Debbie Newell, who vetted the course the prior week, Linda and Elizabeth Hood, who manned the lemonade stand control, and Bruce McAlister, who provided brownies in support of the WIOL program.
Meet Director & Course Setter: Frank Skorina Control Pickup: Holly, Michel, Steve, Liliane Participants: 22

The second-year medical students deliberated whether to compete against each other on the intermediate or advanced course. They had experience with CROC orange level courses and Brett had done at least one green level course. Since they had driven all the way from Portland, they decided to maximize their orienteering and go for the longer advanced course. Brett started two minutes in front of Erik and after meeting up in the woods the friendship developed into high-tempo, head-to-head competition. At the line they finished simultaneously in a near-sprint and were one-two on the advanced course. Chris Howard (along with Penny, his small, but tough dog) rounded out the top three having warmed up with a seven mile run in the morning. Congratulations to the sleep-deprived med students.

Weather, although threatening for a moment, was great and turnout was good considering that this was the first meet of the fall and there was minimal publicity to remind the population of that fact. Most of the participants are members which reflects our membership growth. We are at 32 members (once everyone who promised they will renew does) which is almost 50% higher than our Dec. 15, 1997 level.
Erik and Russell Skorina, Holly Howard
Meet Director & Course Setter: Ivar Husa Control Pickup: Bob Gruel and Michel Billaux Participants: 22

We've come to expect fine weather at this event, and we weren't disappointed. This year the course setter came across a pair of whitetail bucks jousting. What a pretty place to hold an event. Thanks to the competitors who came up from Portland to join us, and we hope you enjoyed our event.
September 27, 1998 Issaquah, WA Sammamish Orienteering Club

Meet Director: Dave Enger Course Designers: Bruce McAlister, Eric Bone Course Setters: Bruce McAlister, Fred Veler Registration: Dave Enger, Kean Williams, Bruce McAlister Timing: Dave Enger Control Pick-up: Bruce McAlister, 4 Engers
October 11, 1998 Shoreline, WA Sammamish Orienteering Club
Meet Director: Dave Enger Course Setter: Jan Urban Registration: Bob Reddick Timing: Jan Urban, Randy Brasfield, Dave Enger Control Pick-up: Debbie Newell, 4 Engers
Meet Director & Course Setter: Ivar Husa Control Pickup: Bob Gruel and Sonny Elsasser Participants: 15

It was as close to a perfect day for orienteering as one could expect. Sunshine, temperatures in the 60's, and no wind. Oh, and two water controls for the longest (8.5km) course! Participants could choose among four courses, a first for our club. We may break down and begin rating them by color, instead of "Beginner, Intermediate, and Advanced".
A note on the map. This was the first use of “Wallula East”, a 1:15000 subset of a larger mapped area, nearly 6 km long! Mapping continues on the south shore.
Steve Weakly, Bill Rutz, Addison, and Alexis
October 25 Woodland Park Cascade Orienteering Club
Meet Director: Robert Miller Assistant Meet Director: Sandy Kish Miller Course Designer and Setter: Jim Siscel Course Pickup: Jim Siscel, Paul Magnus, Bob & Pat Reddick Shadow: Bill Cusworth

This year we had a lovely Fall evening for the Vampire O, no rain and just cool enough to keep the runners from overheating. As in past years, it is utterly unpredictable as to who will come in with the most points. Two fast runners, Paul Magnus and Eric Bone, in the past have been bit many times, losing many points. This year, both runners came prepared with their strategies, Paul wore dark sweats and Eric came in disguise.

Unfortunately Eric's disguise involved sauntering and this lost him valuable minutes. Judging by the times of some participants, I think some runners wanted time to get to some points w/o running into vampires - or perhaps they just were having a great time and didn't want it to end.
Michael Thomas won the award for the best costume. I am not quite sure what he was meant to be, but the costume was suitably horror filled. (I am sure he is really a very nice person.) Robert Miller
Julia Terlinchamp, Rachel Jones, Angela Nitzsche of GS Troop 268
Sue, Tanar, & Aizen Ulric, Janet Flatten, & Rob Cranfill
Boy Scouts Adam Heide, Mickolas Isezy

COC’s Annual Ballot for Board Members

Please select five (5) individuals from the list provided or write in selections of your own choice. In order for your ballot to count, you must print and sign your name in the space provided. You may mail your ballot to the COC address (POB 31375, Seattle, WA 98103) such that it arrives by November 11th or bring it to the annual dinner.
You are welcome to photocopy this form.
